1.pericope_label: The Angel with the Little Scroll
1.exposition: The trumpet series halts. Between the sixth trumpet and the seventh, John is handed an interlude — just as an earlier pause (ch. 7) stood between the sixth and seventh seals. A second mighty angel comes down from heaven, and nearly everything about him is borrowed from the vision of the glorified Christ: wrapped in a cloud, crowned with the rainbow of covenant mercy, his face like the sun and his legs like pillars of fire. He plants his right foot on the sea and his left on the land, laying claim to the whole created order, and cries with a lion's roar — and seven thunders answer him (v.3). John reaches for his pen, and is stopped: seal up what the seven thunders said, and write them not. Then the angel raises his hand and swears by the eternal Creator that there will be no more delay: in the days of the seventh trumpet the mystery of God, long announced to the prophets, will at last be finished (vv.6–7).
1.original_language: The angel is allos angelos ischyros — “another mighty angel” — and his scroll is a biblaridion, a double diminutive (“a little little book”), the small open scroll set deliberately against the great sealed scroll of ch. 5. The crux of the section is the oath's content: chronos ouk estai eti. The KJV renders chronos literally — “there should be time no longer” — but the dominant reading takes it in its idiomatic sense of an interval of waiting: grammatical-historical Robertson translates “no more delay,” no further postponement before the seventh trumpet sounds. The word thus answers the martyrs' “how long?” of 6:10 rather than announcing the abolition of time itself.
1.historical_context: Chapter 10 opens the book's second major interlude, mirroring the sealed-multitude interlude of ch. 7. The angel's regalia — cloud, rainbow, sun-face, fiery limbs — draws on the theophany above the throne in Ezekiel 1:26–28 and on the portrait of the Son of Man in Revelation 1:15–16. His oath uses the full creation formula — the God “who created heaven and the earth and the sea, and all that is in them” (cf. Deuteronomy 32:40; Nehemiah 9:6) — invoking the Creator's authority to guarantee the end. The sealed seven thunders are unique in the book: a revelation deliberately withheld, a counter-current in a work whose very name means unveiling.
1.christ: The oath that the mystery of God will be finished “as he declared to his servants the prophets” (v.7) reaches for a theme the epistles name outright: the mystery hidden for ages, disclosed in the gospel and centered in Christ himself — “Christ in you, the hope of glory” (Colossians 1:27; cf. Romans 16:25–26; Ephesians 3:3–6). And “no more delay” presses toward the seventh trumpet, where the announcement finally comes: “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ” (11:15). The interlude, then, is not a detour but a christological promise — the Lamb's reign is near.

verses.note: A second mighty angel comes down from heaven, clothed with a cloud, a rainbow on his head, his face like the sun and his feet like pillars of fire — and his identity is contested. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson dwell on the surpassing majesty and lean toward a created angel of the highest rank. But the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the figure as Christ himself: the cloud, the covenant rainbow, the sun-face of 1:16, and the fiery feet of 1:15 all echo the vision of the glorified Lord. The debate is old and unresolved; at the least, the angel comes robed in the tokens of divine majesty, mercy, and power.

He holds a little book open — a biblaridion lying open in his hand, set against the sealed scroll the Lamb alone could open in 5:1. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill note the openness signals a revelation now to be made known, later given to John to eat (vv.9–10). Setting his right foot on the sea and his left on the land, the angel bestrides the whole world; the posture claims universal dominion for the One he represents over all the earth.

The angel cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth, and seven thunders answered. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill hear in the lion's roar the note of power, majesty, and warning — the very voice of the prophets (Amos 3:8; Hosea 11:10). The answering “seven thunders” are no mere noise but articulate utterances, complete (the number of fullness) and, as the next verse shows, intelligible to John — which makes their sealing all the more striking.

John understood the thunders and was about to write — obeying his standing commission to record what he saw — when a heavenly voice forbade him: seal them up, and write them not. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott mark this as the one revelation in the book deliberately concealed. Gill draws the pastoral point plainly: we are not to be over-curious about what God has not disclosed, but content with what is made known — for “the secret things belong to the LORD” (Deuteronomy 29:29). Even a book of unveiling keeps a sealed reserve.

The angel standing on sea and land lifted up his hand to heaven. grammatical-historical Robertson and the annotators of the Jamieson-Fausset-Brown commentary identify the gesture at once: the raised hand is the universal posture of the oath, appealing to God in heaven as witness (cf. Daniel 12:7, where the man clothed in linen does the same; Genesis 14:22). The solemn gesture prepares the ear for a declaration of the utmost gravity.

He swore by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven and earth and sea, that there should be “time no longer.” The phrase divides interpreters. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, Gill, and Clarke take it as “no more delay” — no further postponement before the seventh trumpet finishes God's purpose, the answer to the saints' cry of 6:10; a minority hold the surface sense, the literal end of time. Robertson adds a telling point for the identity question of v.1: the angel swears by God, which tells against reading him as Christ, who is sworn by rather than swearing — though the latin-fathers answer that a messenger of Christ, or Christ in mediatorial office, may still invoke the Father.

The substance of the oath: in the days of the seventh angel, the mystery of God should be finished, as he declared to his servants the prophets. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the “mystery of God” as his long-hidden purpose — the whole plan of redemption and the establishing of his kingdom — brought at the seventh trumpet to its full accomplishment. What the prophets announced as good news is here sworn to completion; the consummation fulfills, rather than cancels, the ancient word of God.

witnesses.point: The mighty angel descends in surpassing glory; “time no longer” means no more delay — God is about to consummate his plan at the seventh trumpet.
Renders the oath “no more delay in the fulfillment of the seventh trumpet”; that the angel swears by God tells against identifying him with Christ.
Reads the mighty angel as Christ himself — the cloud, the covenant rainbow, the sun-face, and the fiery feet echo the vision of the glorified Lord in ch. 1.
The sealed thunders teach us not to be over-curious about what God has withheld, but content with what he has revealed.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The angel is portrayed in the colors of the glorified Christ of ch. 1 and of the divine glory of Ezekiel and Daniel, and his oath consciously reworks the oath of Daniel 12:7 — but Revelation transforms Daniel's sealed, deferred prophecy into a word of no more delay.

1.application: God has an unwritten reserve; not everything of his counsel is given us to know, and the seal on the thunders is a mercy, not a withholding of love. Rest, then, in the part he has spoken — and in the oath that his purpose has an appointed end.

8.exposition: The interlude turns from vision to vocation. The same heavenly voice that silenced the thunders now speaks again, sending John to take the little book that is open in the angel's hand. John goes and asks for it, and the angel gives a strange command drawn straight from Ezekiel: take it, and eat it up — it will be sweet as honey in the mouth but bitter in the belly. John obeys, and finds it exactly so (v.10). The eaten scroll is no sooner digested than it becomes a charge: you must prophesy again before many peoples and nations and tongues and kings. The prophet must first receive the word inwardly — assimilate it, taste both its sweetness and its grief — before he can faithfully deliver it to the world.
8.original_language: The angel says labe kai kataphage — “take and eat it up,” devour it whole — so that the scroll becomes part of the prophet. It is glyky hōs meli, “sweet as honey,” in the stoma (mouth), but turns pikron (“bitter”) in the koilia (belly). The closing charge is dei se palin prophēteusai — “it is necessary that you prophesy again,” a divine necessity, not a mere invitation. The little preposition epi in “prophesy epi many peoples” can mean “concerning,” “before,” or even “against” — the renewed commission reaches over the whole sweep of nations, languages, and kings.
8.historical_context: The scene is a prophetic recommissioning modeled directly on Ezekiel 2:9–3:3, where the prophet is handed a scroll written front and back with lament and woe, told to eat it, and finds it “sweet as honey” before being sent to a rebellious house. Jeremiah speaks the same way of ingesting God's words as “the joy and delight of my heart” (Jeremiah 15:16), and the tradition reaches back to Isaiah's burning coal (Isaiah 6:6–7). The fourfold formula — peoples, nations, tongues, kings — is Revelation's signature phrase for the universal scope of the message. John, like the prophets before him, is not merely informed but consecrated.
8.christ: A chain of receiving runs through the book: the Father holds the sealed scroll, the Lamb takes it from his hand (5:7), the mighty angel holds the little open scroll, and now John eats it — the disclosure-chain of 1:1 enacted in visible form, from Father to Lamb to angel to prophet to the churches. What finally makes the bitter scroll sweet is its end: beyond the judgments it requires John to pronounce lies the Lamb's victory and the new creation. The honey in the mouth is the destination — the marriage of the Lamb and the city of God toward which the whole renewed prophecy is bent.

verses.note: The voice from heaven — the same that sealed the thunders (v.4) — speaks again, bidding John go and take the open little book from the angel who stands on sea and land. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that John is not simply shown the scroll but sent to receive it actively; puritan Matthew Henry adds that the prophet must take the message before he can deliver it. The revelation must first be possessed.

John approaches and asks, and the angel answers, take it, and eat it up; it shall make thy belly bitter, but in thy mouth sweet as honey. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill recognize the figure at once from Ezekiel 2:8–3:3: to eat the scroll is to take God's word inwardly, to assimilate the revelation before proclaiming it. Gill notes the two tastes together — the gospel's free grace, pardon, and life are sweeter than honey, yet its prophecies of judgment and the saints' sufferings are bitter to digest.

John does as told: it was in my mouth sweet as honey, and as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter — exactly as the angel foretold. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott dwell on the sequence: the first reception of God's word is pure delight, the joy of the prophetic gift; but once digested, its solemn content — woes to pronounce over nations and kings — turns to grief. The word is sweet to receive and bitter to bear.

The eating equips the eater: thou must prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read this as John's renewed commission after the interlude — the little scroll's contents becoming the substance of the further visions (chs. 11 onward). The fourfold designation marks the universal reach: the message concerns and confronts the whole world, not one people only. The prophet, having taken the word in, is sent back out.

witnesses.point: The scroll is sweet in itself — truth, promise, coming glory — but bitter in delivery, since it involves pronouncing judgments and woes on mankind.
The gospel's doctrines of grace, pardon, and life are sweeter than honey; yet its prophecies of the saints' sufferings and the world's ruin make it bitter in the belly.
John must go and take the book — the prophet must receive and inwardly digest the message before he can faithfully deliver it.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The little scroll is the content of John's own prophecy — the message he is recommissioned to proclaim from chapter 11 onward; its sweetness is the good news of God's kingdom, its bitterness the suffering and judgment through which that kingdom comes.

8.application: God's word is meant to be eaten, not merely examined — taken so far inward that it becomes part of you before it passes your lips to others. And faithful witness will taste of both: honey in the joy of the gospel, gall in the cost of speaking it truly. Do not be surprised when the same word both gladdens and grieves you.

_meta.title: The Mighty Angel and the Little Scroll
reflection: The seven thunders spoke, yet John was told to “seal them up” and not write them (v.4). What does it mean that even in a book of unveiling God keeps some things hidden (Deuteronomy 29:29)? Where are you tempted to demand knowledge God has not given?
The angel swears there will be “no more delay” (v.6), answering the martyrs' cry of “how long?” (6:10). How does the promise that God's purpose has an appointed end steady you in seasons of waiting?
The little scroll was sweet in John's mouth but bitter in his belly (vv.9–10). Have you known God's word to be at once delight and burden? What makes faithful proclamation of it costly?
